title: DSpinGNN: A Physics-Informed Equivariant Graph Neural Network for Dynamic Magnetic Exchange Prediction in Strain-Deformed Monolayer CrI$_3$
authors: Isam A. Balghari, M. Faryad, M. Sabieh Anwar
abstract: Resolving the instantaneous, position-dependent isotropic magnetic exchange coupling $J_{ij}$ across a dynamically deforming crystal lattice requires a computational approach that simultaneously handles structural forces and magnetic interactions at length scales inaccessible to first-principles methods. Here we introduce DSpinGNN, a bifurcated machine-learning architecture comprising an $E(3)$-equivariant graph neural network (E-GNN) for classical Langevin structural dynamics and a physics-informed $Δ$-MLP that maps instantaneous local Cr-I-Cr bond geometry to isotropic exchange couplings, with the Goodenough-Kanamori superexchange relationship embedded as an analytical inductive bias. Trained on 345 DFT+U configurations of monolayer CrI$_3$ and evaluated on a strictly withheld 61-configuration test set, DSpinGNN simultaneously achieves an energy MAE of $1.1$ meV/atom, a force MAE of $6.5$ meV/Å, and an exchange coupling MAE of $0.18$ meV ($R^2 = 0.91$). Deployed at 400$\times$ scale in a 3,200-atom supercell under a collinear Ising-constrained adiabatic approximation at $5$ K, the model maps the local exchange response to a propagating biaxial strain wave. Wave reflection at periodic boundaries generates transient constructive interference regions where local compressive strain exceeds the DFT-established FM-to-AFM threshold, producing spatially heterogeneous exchange coupling textures that damp as the wave dissipates. Quantitative analysis yields a domain wall width of $ξ= 1.7 \pm 0.3$~nm and a constructive-interference oscillation period of $τ= 0.27$~ps -- mesoscopic observables inaccessible to direct DFT and constituting testable predictions for cryogenic magnetic force microscopy. DSpinGNN provides a reproducible, transferable framework for mesoscale exchange mapping in strain-driven 2D magnetic materials.
summary: This work introduces DSpinGNN, an advanced machine learning model that predicts strain-dependent magnetic exchange couplings in monolayer CrI$_3$. By combining equivariant graph networks with physics constraints derived from the Goodenough-Kanamori rules, it accurately models how mechanical strain generates spatially heterogeneous magnetic textures. The resulting framework provides testable mesoscopic predictions for cryogenic magnetic measurements.
main_problem: The core challenge is accurately calculating the instantaneous, position-dependent isotropic magnetic exchange coupling ($J_{ij}$) across a crystal lattice undergoing dynamic structural deformation.
main_result: DSpinGNN successfully predicts these couplings on unseen data and simulates strain wave propagation, yielding testable mesoscopic predictions like domain wall width ($\xi=1.7 	ext{ nm}$) and oscillation period ($	au=0.27 	ext{ ps}$).
method: A bifurcated machine-learning architecture combines an E(3)-equivariant GNN for structural dynamics with a physics-informed $\Delta$-MLP that maps local bond geometry to $J_{ij}$, incorporating the Goodenough-Kanamori superexchange relationship.
model_or_system: The system is monolayer CrI$_3$, modeled using an effective classical Heisenberg Hamiltonian ($H_{	ext{spin}}$). The framework operates under several approximations, including the collinear Ising limit and adiabatic decoupling between structure and magnetism.
key_observables: Energy MAE (1.1 meV/atom), Force MAE (6.5 meV/Å), Exchange Coupling MAE (0.18 meV), Domain wall width ($\xi$), Oscillation period ($	au$).
important_parameters: Temperature ($T=5 	ext{ K}$), Strain magnitude (up to $\pm 9.1\%$ compression causing FM-to-AFM transition).
main_assumptions: The model assumes an adiabatic decoupling between structural dynamics and magnetic coupling, and it is restricted to the collinear isotropic limit by omitting SOC effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the workflow from DFT data generation to ML prediction; key results show $J_{ij}$ landscapes during strain wave reflection, confirming the formation of transient AFM-sign zones. Quantitative analysis provides fitted values for $\xi$ and $	au$.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the computational difficulty (instantaneous $J_{ij}$). It then details the DSpinGNN architecture—the E-GNN structural branch and the physics-informed $\Delta$-MLP exchange predictor. The methodology is validated on a test set, followed by mesoscale simulations of strain wave dynamics to extract quantitative physical observables.
why_interesting: The integration of equivariant ML directly into predicting magnetic interactions under mechanical stress provides a powerful, transferable tool for simulating complex quantum materials behavior at mesoscopic scales, bridging solid-state theory and AI.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
